title: Views about AI's impact on society in the next 20 years
subtitle: Survey respondents were asked, "Will artificial intelligence help or harm people in the next 20 years?"
caption: In 2021, survey respondents across eleven countries shared their expectations regarding whether artificial intelligence will help or harm people over the next twenty years, with answers distributed across five categories: "Mostly help," "Neither," "No response," "No opinion or don't know," and "Mostly harm." The data, presented as a stacked horizontal bar chart, reveals a wide divergence in public sentiment, ranging from high optimism in East Asia to significant concern in South Asia. Japan and China show the highest levels of optimism, with 61% of respondents in both nations selecting "Mostly help," while only 13% in each country anticipate that AI will "Mostly harm" society. Germany follows closely with 60% expecting a positive impact, though 18% there foresee harm. In contrast, Pakistan displays the lowest level of optimism, where only 19% believe AI will mostly help, and a majority of 52% expect it will mostly harm. Indonesia and India also show lower confidence in AI's benefits, with "Mostly help" figures at 22% and 33% respectively, while the "No opinion or don't know" category reaches its highest point in Indonesia at 44%. The United States and France sit in the middle of the distribution, with 35% and 40% respectively expecting AI to help, matched by equal or higher proportions expecting harm or holding no opinion. Italy and the United Kingdom show split views, with half or fewer respondents expecting a net positive outcome. The chart illustrates a clear geographic gradient in expectations, moving from predominantly positive outlooks in East and Central Europe to predominantly negative or uncertain outlooks in South and Southeast Asia.
